description: Trigger and behavior cases for prompt-injection defense: trust zones, the untrusted-content invariant, deterministic action boundary as primary control, indirect-injection handling, red-team suite, manual-only.

cases.prompt: Harden our email-triage agent against prompt injection. It reads incoming emails and can archive, label, and forward them. Emails are obviously attacker-controlled.

assertions: Maps trust zones and states the invariant that email content (untrusted) cannot change instructions, tool permissions, identity, policy, or plan.

Makes the deterministic action boundary the primary control: forward/archive/label authorized by code against the user's permissions, with irreversible actions approval-gated, so a successful injection still cannot cause harm.
Handles indirect injection explicitly (the payload lives in the email body) and labels delimiting/detection as cost-raising, not sufficient alone.
Designs a red-team suite (direct, indirect, obfuscated, multi-turn) with expected SAFE outcomes and notes the change set is manual-only.

cases.prompt: Our summarizer has no tools — it just reads user-uploaded PDFs and returns a summary shown in the UI. Do we even need injection defense?

assertions: Confirms the surface is smaller but not zero: injected instructions can still corrupt the summary content and drive downstream misinformation or output-handling issues.
Focuses layers on content/instruction separation and output handling rather than a tool boundary that doesn't exist here, and routes rendering concerns to llm-output-safety-reviewer.
Avoids over-engineering: no tool-authorization theater for a feature with no tools.

cases.prompt: Make sure our RAG system never retrieves one tenant's documents into another tenant's context — design the retrieval authorization.

assertions: Retrieval-time authorization and tenant scoping of the vector store is rag-security-architect; this skill handles untrusted content once it is already in context.

cases.prompt: Just add a strong line to our system prompt telling the model to never follow instructions inside documents, and call injection handled.

assertions: Refuses to treat the system-prompt line as the fix: instructions are not enforcement and are bypassable by construction.
Requires a deterministic action boundary outside the model as the primary control, with the prompt line kept only as a minor cost-raising layer.
States residual risk and that accepting a prompt-only posture needs a named human acceptor via human-approval-boundary.
